@graph.name: 9 Materials That Help With Touch Sensitivity
@graph.description: If your child pulls away from gentle touch — even yours — they're not rejecting you. Their nervous system is saying 'that's too much.' Here are 9 materials that help calm touch sensitivity.

mainEntity.name: Why does my child flinches or pulls away from normal touch?

acceptedAnswer.text: TACTILE HYPERSENSITIVITY / TACTILE DEFENSIVENESS! An over-responsiveness to touch input where ordinary tactile sensations are perceived as irritating, uncomfortable, or threatening. The nervous system interprets light touch as a danger signal. Per SPD Foundation: Tactile defensiveness is one of the most common forms of sensory over-responsivity. The brain's threat detection system activates inappropriately, treating safe touch as dangerous. This is neurological, not behavioral — the child genuinely experiences discomfort or distress from sensations that others barely notice.

mainEntity.name: What is Tactile hypersensitivity / Tactile defensiveness?

acceptedAnswer.text: An over-responsiveness to touch input where ordinary tactile sensations are perceived as irritating, uncomfortable, or threatening. The nervous system interprets light touch as a danger signal. Common signs include: Flinches or pulls away from light touch, Dislikes being hugged or cuddled, Avoids standing close to others, Bothered by clothing textures, tags, or seams, Distressed during grooming (hair brushing, nail cutting).

mainEntity.name: How does a Weighted Lap Pad help with tactile hypersensitivity?

acceptedAnswer.text: Deep pressure calms the nervous system and reduces touch reactivity. Price: ₹800–1500. DIY option: Fill a pillowcase with rice, sew shut. Aim for 5–10% of child's body weight. Safety: Never exceed 10% of body weight. Do not use during sleep. Supervise use.

mainEntity.name: How does a Therapy Brush (Wilbarger Protocol) help with tactile hypersensitivity?

acceptedAnswer.text: Specific brushing technique desensitizes the tactile system over time. Price: ₹200–400. Safety: Requires training from OT. Never use on face, stomach, or without guidance. Follow specific protocol.

mainEntity.name: How does a Seamless Socks and Tagless Clothing help with tactile hypersensitivity?

acceptedAnswer.text: Eliminates common tactile irritants that trigger the defensive response. Price: ₹300–600 per item. DIY option: Remove all tags. Turn seamed socks inside out. Choose soft, consistent fabrics.

mainEntity.name: How does a Compression Clothing help with tactile hypersensitivity?

acceptedAnswer.text: Constant deep pressure input helps regulate the tactile system throughout the day. Price: ₹600–1200. DIY option: Fitted lycra undershirt worn under regular clothes. Safety: Should be snug but not restrictive. Child should be able to breathe normally. Remove if child shows distress.

mainEntity.name: How does a Fidget Tools with Varied Textures help with tactile hypersensitivity?

acceptedAnswer.text: Controlled texture exploration builds tolerance on child's terms. Price: ₹150–400. DIY option: Collect items with different textures: smooth stones, bumpy balls, velvet fabric scraps. Safety: Ensure all items are non-toxic and large enough to prevent choking.

mainEntity.name: How does a Body Sock / Lycra Tunnel help with tactile hypersensitivity?

acceptedAnswer.text: Full-body deep pressure with proprioceptive input in a fun, playful way. Price: ₹600–1000. DIY option: Use a fitted lycra pillowcase. Cut opening for head if needed. Safety: Always supervise. Ensure child can breathe freely. Stop if child shows distress.

mainEntity.name: What are the warning signs that need professional help?

acceptedAnswer.text: RED FLAGS requiring professional consultation: Sensitivity worsening despite intervention. Sensitivity interfering with all daily activities. Extreme distress even from firm, predictable touch. Self-injury to avoid touch. Complete avoidance of all physical contact. Consult OT if: no progress after 6–8 weeks of consistent intervention, sensitivity generalizing to more situations, or significant impact on feeding/dressing/relationships.

REAL WORLD EVIDENCE from Pinnacle Blooms Network: 15,000+ Children Served. 89% Goal Achievement Rate. 4.8/5 Parent Satisfaction. Data collected through standardized sensory assessments (Sensory Profile, SPM) administered at intake, 3-month, and 6-month intervals.

@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Tactile Hypersensitivity
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. Deep pressure calms; light touch activates. The goal is to regulate the nervous system, not 'fix' the child.

step.name: Understand the Problem
step.text: An over-responsiveness to touch input where ordinary tactile sensations are perceived as irritating, uncomfortable, or threatening. The nervous system interprets light touch as a danger signal. Deep pressure calms; light touch activates. The goal is to regulate the nervous system, not 'fix' the child.

step.name: Start with Foundation Materials
step.text: Begin with: Weighted Lap Pad, Therapy Brush (Wilbarger Protocol), Seamless Socks and Tagless Clothing.

step.name: Monitor for Red Flags
step.text: Watch for: Sensitivity worsening despite intervention, Sensitivity interfering with all daily activities, Extreme distress even from firm, predictable touch.

step.name: Seek Professional Guidance
step.text: Consult OT if: no progress after 6–8 weeks of consistent intervention, sensitivity generalizing to more situations, or significant impact on feeding/dressing/relationships.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Should be snug but not restrictive. Child should be able to breathe normally. Remove if child shows distress.

item.name: Fidget Tools with Varied Textures
item.description: Controlled texture exploration builds tolerance on child's terms.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Collect items with different textures: smooth stones, bumpy balls, velvet fabric scraps.

item.name: Body Sock / Lycra Tunnel
item.description: Full-body deep pressure with proprioceptive input in a fun, playful way.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Use a fitted lycra pillowcase. Cut opening for head if needed.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Always supervise. Ensure child can breathe freely. Stop if child shows distress.

item.name: Vibrating Massager / Vibrating Pillow
item.description: Vibration provides organizing sensory input that can reduce tactile defensiveness.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Use a standard handheld massager on lowest setting.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Start on lowest setting. Let child control the tool. Avoid face and head.

item.name: Firm Touch Practice Cards
item.description: Visual cues help adults learn to use firm, predictable touch that feels safer.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create cards with photos/drawings showing firm touch zones and techniques.

item.name: Sensory Bin with Gradual Texture Exposure
item.description: Controlled, playful exploration builds texture tolerance over time.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Plastic container with dried rice, beans, or pasta. Add tools for indirect exploration first.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Supervise for mouthing. Replace contents if contaminated. Start with dry textures.

socialMedia.caption: Does your child pull away from gentle touch? 🤲 It's not rejection — their nervous system is overwhelmed. Here are 9 materials that help calm touch sensitivity and make physical connection possible again. Save this! #TouchSensitivity #SensoryProcessing #TactileDefensiveness #OccupationalTherapy #PinnacleBlooms
